author: phase-4-coordinator
content:
Resolved via option 3 from the issue (remove the misleading SIMD dispatch). The four fake-SIMD wrappers (UnaryF32Op, UnaryF64Op, BinaryF32Op, BinaryF64Op) were deleted entirely — their `impl pulp::WithSimd for ... { fn with_simd<S>(_simd: S) { scalar loop } }` bodies ignored the SIMD parameter and ran plain scalar loops on every ISA. The dispatch_unary_f32/f64 and dispatch_binary_f32/f64 entry points are collapsed into direct scalar loops with a comment pointing at this issue so the next reader knows why the SIMD trappings are gone.

All the GENUINE SIMD kernels (SqrtF64Op, AbsF64Op, NegF64Op, SquareF64Op, ReciprocalF64Op, SqrtF32Op, ExpFastF64Op, ExpFastF32Op) are untouched — those use real pulp intrinsics via simd.sqrt_f64s and friends. The section header for the real-SIMD block is updated to note that the fake-SIMD wrappers used to sit above it.

Public API is unchanged: dispatch_unary_f32/f64 and dispatch_binary_f32/f64 keep the same signature, so a future vectorized transcendental library (sleef-rs / libmvec / hand-rolled pulp) can swap back in without breaking callers. This is tracked as the bigger #393 issue.

255 lib tests + 50/19/53 integration tests still pass — no regression since the SIMD path was already scalar. Clippy clean. Net effect: ~80 lines of dead code deleted, and the misleading comment claiming pulp-based SIMD dispatch is replaced with honest scalar-loop documentation.
